instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: Decidability 01
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul
tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 01
Decidability
A language is decidable if there is a decider decides it
A language is a set of strings
A decider is a Turing machine that halts on all inputs

runs.text: is a Turing machine that halts on all inputs

text_blocks.text: A Turing machine halts when it enters either the accept state

runs.text: when it enters either the accept state

text_blocks.text: (qaccept) or the reject state (qreject)
To show that a language A is decidable:
Construct a TM M that you think it decides the language A

runs.text: that you think it decides the language

text_blocks.text: and prove that
For every s ∈A, M must accept s and
For every s ̸∈A, M must reject s
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 01
Decidability
We constructed M and proved that
For every s ∈A, M must accept s and
For every s ̸∈A, M must reject s
This allows us to conclude the following:

runs.text: This allows us to conclude the following:

text_blocks.text: L(M) = A
M accepts all strings in A and does not accept all strings not

runs.text: and does not accept all strings not

text_blocks.text: in A
M is a decider (always halts)
Given a string s, it is either in A or not in A
If s ∈A, M accepts s (halts)
If s ̸∈A, M rejects s (halts)
A is decidable
The decider M decides A

is decidable.

M =“On input ⟨B, w⟩, where B is a DFA and w is a string:
Simulate B on input w.
If the simulation ends in an accept state, accept. If it ends in a nonaccepting

text_blocks.text: state, reject.”

Decidability 01
Decidable Languages
ANFA
Let ANFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is an NFA that accepts input string w}.

runs.text: is an NFA that accepts input string

text_blocks.text: Show that ANFA is decidable.
Again, to show that ANFA is decidable, we need to construct

runs.text: is decidable, we need to construct

text_blocks.text: a TM N and prove the following:
If ⟨B, w⟩∈ANFA, N accepts ⟨B, w⟩
If ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ANFA, N rejects ⟨B, w⟩
As discussed earlier in Chapter 1, there is an algorithm that

runs.text: As discussed earlier in Chapter 1, there is an algorithm that

text_blocks.text: allows us convert an NFA B into an equivalent DFA C
Since there is an algorithm, a Turing machine that convert an

runs.text: Since there is an algorithm, a Turing machine that convert an

text_blocks.text: NFA B into a equivalent DFA C where L(B) = L(C)
If NFA B accepts w, DFA C also accepts w
If DFA C accepts w, ⟨C, w⟩∈ADFA
If ⟨C, w⟩∈ADFA, TM M (decider for ADFA) accept ⟨C, w⟩

Decidable Languages
TM N can be constructed as follows:
N =“On input ⟨B, w⟩, where B is a NFA and w is a string:
Convert NFA B to an equivalent DFA C, using the procedure
for this conversion given in Theorem 1.39.

runs.text: for this conversion given in Theorem 1.39.

text_blocks.text: Run TM M from Theorem 4.1 on input ⟨C, w⟩.
If M accepts, accept; otherwise, reject.”
Notes about TM N
Recall that the language of TM M is ADFA:

runs.text: Recall that the language of TM

text_blocks.text: ADFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is a DFA that accepts w}
TM N runs TM M on input ⟨C, w⟩where
C is a DFA that it just constructed from NFA B

runs.text: is a DFA that it just constructed from NFA

text_blocks.text: w is a string
M will accept or reject ⟨C, w⟩depending on whether ⟨C, w⟩
in ADFA
TM N runs TM M on input ⟨B, w⟩, M will always reject the
input since B is an NFA
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 01
Prove that ANFA is Decidable
Prove that ANFA = {⟨B, w⟩| B is an NFA that accepts w} is decidable.
Solution: Construct a TM N as follows:
N =“On input ⟨B, w⟩, where B is a NFA and w is a string:
Convert NFA B to an equivalent DFA C, using the procedure for this conversion

runs.text: , using the procedure for this conversion

text_blocks.text: given in Theorem 1.39.
Run TM M from Theorem 4.1 on input ⟨C, w⟩.
If M accepts ⟨C, w⟩, accept; otherwise, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that N decides ANFA:

runs.text: Next, we need to prove that

text_blocks.text: Case 1: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩∈ANFA. Since ⟨B, w⟩∈ANFA, by the deﬁnition
of the language ANFA, B is an NFA that accepts the string w. By converting

runs.text: is an NFA that accepts the string

text_blocks.text: the NFA B into an equivalent DFA C (L(B) = L(C)), C also accepts the string
w. Since C is a DFA that accepts the string w, ⟨C, w⟩∈ADFA. Since M is a

runs.text: is a DFA that accepts the string

text_blocks.text: decider for ADFA, by running M on input ⟨C, w⟩, M will accept ⟨C, w⟩which
will cause N to accept ⟨B, w⟩.
Case 2: Assume that ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ANFA, Since ⟨B, w⟩̸∈ANFA, by the deﬁnition
of the language ANFA, B is an NFA that rejects the string w. By converting

runs.text: is an NFA that rejects the string

text_blocks.text: the NFA B into an equivalent DFA C (L(B) = L(C)), C also rejects the string
w. Since C is a DFA that rejects the string w, ⟨C, w⟩̸∈ADFA. Since M is a

runs.text: is a DFA that rejects the string

text_blocks.text: decider for ADFA, by running M on input ⟨C, w⟩, M will reject ⟨C, w⟩which
will cause N to reject ⟨B, w⟩.
This shows that TM N is a decider for ANFA. Therefore, ANFA is decidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 01
Decidable Language
AREX
Let
AREX = {⟨R, w⟩| R is a regular expression that generates string w}.

runs.text: is a regular expression that generates string

text_blocks.text: Show that AREX is decidable.
Again, to show that AREX is decidable, we need to construct

runs.text: is decidable, we need to construct

text_blocks.text: a TM P and prove the following:
If ⟨R, w⟩∈AREX, P accepts ⟨R, w⟩
If ⟨R, w⟩̸∈AREX, P rejects ⟨R, w⟩
Recall that we have an algorithm that generates an NFA A

runs.text: Recall that we have an algorithm that generates an NFA

text_blocks.text: from a regular expression R where the language of A is
expressed by R
Again, since there is an algorithm, we can have a Turing

runs.text: Again, since there is an algorithm, we can have a Turing

text_blocks.text: machine that convert a regular expression R into an equivalent

runs.text: machine that convert a regular expression

text_blocks.text: NFA A
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 01
Prove that AREX is Decidable
Prove that AREX = {⟨R, w⟩| R is a regular expression that generates string w} is

runs.text: is a regular expression that generates string

text_blocks.text: decidable.
Solution: Construct a TM P as follows:
P =“On input ⟨R, w⟩, where R is a regular express and w is a string:
Convert regular expression R to an equivalent NFA A by using the procedure for
this conversion given in Theorem 1.54.
Run TM N from Theorem 4.2 on input ⟨A, w⟩.
If N accepts ⟨A, w⟩, accept; if N rejects ⟨A, w⟩, reject.”
Next, we need to prove that P decides AREX:

runs.text: Next, we need to prove that

text_blocks.text: Case 1: Assume that ⟨R, w⟩∈AREX. Since ⟨R, w⟩∈AREX, from the
deﬁnition of AREX, R is a regular expression that generates the string w. Since

runs.text: is a regular expression that generates the string

text_blocks.text: R is a regular expression that generates w, by converting R into an equivalent

runs.text: is a regular expression that generates

text_blocks.text: NFA A, A accepts w. Since NFA A accepts w, ⟨A, w⟩∈ANFA. Since TM N is
a decider for ANFA, by running N on input ⟨A, w⟩, N will accept ⟨A, w⟩which
causes P to accept ⟨R, w⟩.
Case 2: Assume that ⟨R, w⟩̸∈AREX. Since ⟨R, w⟩̸∈AREX, from the
deﬁnition of AREX, R is a regular expression that does not generate the string

runs.text: is a regular expression that does not generate the string

text_blocks.text: w. Since R is a regular expression that does not generate w, by converting R

runs.text: is a regular expression that does not generate

text_blocks.text: into an equivalent NFA A, A rejects w. Since NFA A rejects w,
⟨A, w⟩̸∈ANFA. Since TM N is a decider for ANFA, by running N on input
⟨A, w⟩, N will reject ⟨A, w⟩which causes P to reject ⟨R, w⟩.
This shows that TM P is a decider for AREX. Therefore, AREX is decidable.
Thumrongsak Kosiyatrakul tkosiyat@cs.pitt.edu
Decidability 01
